alignments.preprint_claim_text: In-situ inference results in an energy dissipation of only 13 pJ per inference after training on the MNIST dataset for 10 epochs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our proposed approach demonstrates a possibility of at least two orders of magnitude energy savings compared to the floating-point approach implemented in CMOS.
alignments.rationale: The preprint gives a specific energy-per-inference number, while the publication reframes this as a broader claim of large (two orders of magnitude) energy savings; the numerical specificity is dropped and the claim is presented more strongly in comparative terms.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest inference accuracies obtained after in-situ and ex-situ training are approximately 96.67% and 96.63%, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds concrete numerical accuracy results that were not stated as specific numbers in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Large inter-state intervals due to quantized weights and noise tolerance margin enable in-situ training with significantly lower number of programming attempts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed Resilient and Adaptive Data-driven alert and Attention Management Strategy (RADAMS) outperforms the default strategy and can reduce the IDoS risk by as much as 20%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Resilient and Adaptive Data-driven alert and Attention Management Strategy (RADAMS) can reduce the IDoS risk by as much as 20%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert up to a 20% reduction in IDoS risk; wording in the publication is slightly more concise but conveys the same result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RADAMS is resilient to large variations of costs, attack frequencies, and human attention capacities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RADAMS is resilient to large variations of costs, attack frequencies, and human attention capacities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The resilience claims are the same in both documents with matching scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integrated modeling and theoretical analysis lead to the Product Principle of Attention (PPoA), fundamental limits, and the tradeoff among crucial human and economic factors. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integrated modeling and theoretical analysis lead to the Product Principle of Attention (PPoA) and fundamental limits. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains PPoA and fundamental limits but omits the explicit mention of the tradeoff among human and economic factors, narrowing the claim's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We have recognized interesting phenomena such as attentional risk equivalency, attacker's dilemma, and the half-truth optimal attack strategy. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: These specific phenomena are described in the preprint but do not appear in the publication's claim list, so the claim appears removed from the final claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI ethics must move beyond race-based representation and bias to examine deeper relations impacting the design, development, and deployment of these systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI ethics must move beyond race-based representation and bias to examine deeper relations impacting the design, development, and deployment of these systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The central normative conclusion is identical in wording and intent between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Antiblackness in AI requires an examination of the ontological space that underpins the design, development, and deployment of AI systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Antiblackness in AI requires an examination of the ontological space that underpins the design, development, and deployment of AI systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim about examining ontological space with respect to antiblackness is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The paper discusses results from auditing an existing open-source semantic network (ConceptNet) to illustrate antiblackness in the context of de-biasing efforts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The article discusses results from auditing an existing open-source semantic network (ConceptNet) to illustrate antiblackness in AI systems. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the audit result but slightly tones down certainty (high -> medium) and frames the illustration more generally, so the claim is clarified and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The authors suggest questions to consider when attempting to combat antiblackness in AI systems. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The authors suggest questions to consider when attempting to combat antiblackness in AI systems. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The set of suggested questions appears verbatim and unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Oracle Teacher model provides the student with more optimal guidance by leveraging both source inputs and output labels. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Oracle Teacher model provides the student with more optimal guidance by leveraging both source inputs and output labels. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The core contribution about the Oracle Teacher model's guidance is the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed training strategy effectively prevents the trivial solution of the model's output directly copying the target input. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed training strategy effectively prevents the trivial solution of the model's output directly copying the target input. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The prevention of the trivial copying solution is asserted identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model improves the students in speech recognition and scene text recognition tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model improves the students in speech recognition and scene text recognition tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance improvement claims for the student models are unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model achieves a considerable speed-up in the teacher model's training time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model achieves a considerable speed-up in the teacher model's training time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Reported training speed-up is maintained verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed semi-supervised learning method for UC classification efficiently extracts essential information by utilizing the location in the colon and image capturing order. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed semi-supervised learning method for ulcerative colitis classification efficiently extracts essential information by utilizing the location in the colon and image capturing order. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The method description is effectively identical, aside from spelling out UC as ulcerative colitis in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms several existing semi-supervised learning methods in the classification task. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms several existing semi-supervised learning methods in the classification task. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental superiority claim is carried over verbatim to the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method is effective even with a small number of annotated images. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method is effective even with a small number of annotated images. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Effectiveness with few annotations is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transformers outperformed other architectures in Neural Machine Translation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transformers outperformed other architectures in Neural Machine Translation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim verbatim, with no change in scope or expressed confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transformers with more encoders and decoders took more time to train but had fewer BLEU scores. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transformers with more encoders and decoders took more time to train but had fewer BLEU scores. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same comparative training-time and BLEU finding with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LSTM performed well in the experiment and took comparatively less time to train than transformers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LSTM performed well in the experiment and took comparatively less time to train than transformers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental result about LSTM is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of transformers is limited by their high computational resource requirements. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this explicit limitation claim about transformers; instead it adds a different conclusion about LSTM suitability (Q3).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed generator can synthesize a dataset of realistic looking microbiological images for training deep learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed generator can synthesize a dataset of realistic looking microbiological images for training deep learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-confidence result with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Starting with only 100 real images, the method can generate data to train a detector that achieves comparable results to a detector trained on a real dataset containing over 7k images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Starting with only 100 real images, the method can generate data to train a detector that achieves comparable results to a detector trained on a real dataset containing over 7,000 images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the quantitative data-generation and detector-comparison claim with identical meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method requires significantly fewer resources to obtain a useful dataset than collecting and labeling a large set of real images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method requires significantly fewer resources to obtain a useful dataset than collecting and labeling a large set of real images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The resource-efficiency conclusion is carried over without change in wording or emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method is expected to be general and flexible, applicable in other domains of science and industry for object detection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method is expected to be general and flexible, applicable in other domains of science and industry for object detection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the generalizability claim with the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework with a multi-task CNN model achieves accuracies of around 90%, which is 6% better than the best previous method examined. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed framework with a multi-task CNN model achieves accuracies of around 90% for assessing MRI image quality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the 90% accuracy claim but separates the comparative improvement into a different claim (Q1) and clarifies the application (MRI image quality).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model outperforms human experts by 3% on noise assessment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrates a 6% improvement over the best previous method and a 3% improvement over human experts in noise assessment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication combines the human-expert improvement with the comparative improvement over prior methods in one claim, clarifying the full set of performance gains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Label calibration, image rulers, and multi-task training improve the model's performance and generalizability. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Label calibration, image rulers, and multi-task training significantly enhance the model's performance and generalizability. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication uses stronger wording ('significantly enhance') to describe these components' effect, increasing the asserted strength of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Existing IQA methods for MRI provide only a general quality score, which is agnostic to the cause of and solution to low-quality scans. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Existing IQA methods for MRI provide only a general quality score without addressing the causes of low-quality scans. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is expressed similarly, but the publication reduces the stated certainty (high -> medium), indicating a tempered presentation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach effectively retrieves speaker information from personalized speaker adapted neural network acoustic models in automatic speech recognition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach effectively retrieves speaker information from personalized speaker adapted neural network acoustic models in automatic speech recognition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same effectiveness claim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two attack models were developed to infer speaker identity from updated personalized models without access to actual users' speech data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two attack models were developed to infer speaker identity from updated personalized models without access to actual users’ speech data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experiments on the TED-LIUM 3 corpus demonstrate that the proposed approaches can achieve an equal error rate (EER) of 1-2%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experiments on the TED-LIUM 3 corpus demonstrate that the proposed approaches can achieve an equal error rate (EER) of 1–2%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental EER result is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithm achieves the optimal dynamic regret of \tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(V_T^{2/5}T^{3/5}\right) for controlling an LQR system with unknown and non-stationary dynamics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithm achieves the optimal dynamic regret of O(V_T^2/5 T^3/5) for controlling a Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) system with unknown and non-stationary dynamics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same theoretical regret bound for the LQR setting with equivalent notation and no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: With piece-wise constant dynamics, the algorithm achieves the optimal regret of \tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{ST}) where S is the number of switches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: With piecewise constant dynamics, the algorithm achieves the optimal regret of O(sqrtST) where S is the number of switches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The piecewise-constant dynamics result is preserved in the publication with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Non-adaptive forgetting strategies may not be regret optimal for the LQR problem, even with optimally tuned window sizes. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Non-adaptive forgetting strategies may not be regret optimal for the LQR problem, even with optimally tuned window sizes. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation regarding non-adaptive forgetting strategies is included unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis of the algorithm reveals that the ordinary least squares estimator has a small bias when estimating non-stationary parameters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis of the algorithm reveals that the ordinary least squares (OLS) estimator has a small bias when estimating non-stationary parameters. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the OLS-bias analytical finding but presents it with lower stated certainty (high -> medium), indicating a tempered presentation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LQR problem exhibits characteristics of a bandit problem with linear feedback and locally quadratic cost, suggesting broader applicability of the results. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LQR problem exhibits characteristics of a bandit problem with linear feedback and locally quadratic cost, suggesting broader applicability of the results. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim about broader applicability is preserved without meaningful change.
